title: Area of Rectangles Using Multiplication
essential_question: How can you use multiplication to find the area of a rectangle?

pretest.question: A rectangle is 5 cm long and 3 cm wide. What is its area? (Write the number of sq cm.)

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L3_area_multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

pretest.question: How do you find the area of a rectangle?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L3_area_multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

pretest.question: A rectangle is 7 m long and 4 m wide. Area in sq m?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L3_area_multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

pretest.question: A square has sides 6 cm. Area in sq cm?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L3_area_multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

feedback.incorrect: A square's area = side × side = 6 × 6 = 36 sq cm.

pretest.question: Sara's rug is 8 ft by 5 ft. What is its area?

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L3_area_multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Why Multiplication Works
learn.content: When you cover a rectangle with unit squares, you make rows AND columns. The total = rows × squares-per-row. A rectangle with LENGTH L and WIDTH W has L × W unit squares — so its area = L × W.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L3_area_multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Length and Width
learn.content: Rectangles have a LENGTH (the longer side, usually) and a WIDTH (the shorter side). Some books also say SIDE LENGTHS. To find area, you need both side lengths.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L3_area_multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Area Formula for Rectangle
learn.content: FORMULA: Area = Length × Width (or A = L × W). Example: a 6 cm by 4 cm rectangle has area = 6 × 4 = 24 sq cm. The formula is a shortcut so we don't have to count every unit square.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L3_area_multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Area Formula for Square
learn.content: A square is a special rectangle where length = width. So Area = side × side. Example: a square with side 5 cm has area = 5 × 5 = 25 sq cm.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L3_area_multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Units in Area
learn.content: When sides are in cm, area is in sq cm. When sides are in m, area is in sq m. ALWAYS write the unit with the area: 24 sq cm, 50 sq m, 120 sq ft. Don't mix units (don't multiply 5 cm × 3 m).

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L3_area_multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Order Doesn't Matter
learn.content: Length × Width = Width × Length. A 4×7 rectangle has the same area as a 7×4 rectangle: both are 28 sq units. This is the COMMUTATIVE property.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L3_area_multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Find a Missing Side
learn.content: If you know the area and one side, you can find the other side by dividing. Example: a rectangle has area 24 sq cm and length 6 cm. Width = 24 ÷ 6 = 4 cm.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L3_area_multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

learn.title: Lesson Summary
learn.content: Today: (1) Area of rectangle = Length × Width. (2) Area of square = side × side. (3) Always include 'square' units. (4) Order doesn't matter (4×7 = 7×4). (5) To find a missing side: Area ÷ known side.

verification.concept_source: Go Math Grade 3 Ch.12 Lesson — U12_area/L3_area_multiplication
verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

try.question: Tom's table is 3 ft by 5 ft. Lily's table is 4 ft by 4 ft. Whose table has BIGGER area?

feedback.correct: Right! Lily's 16 sq ft > Tom's 15 sq ft.
feedback.incorrect: Lily's table (4×4=16 sq ft) is bigger than Tom's (3×5=15 sq ft).

feedback.incorrect: True. Example: 2×6 and 3×4 both have area 12.

practice_r3.question: Sara's garden is 7 m by 4 m. She wants to double the area by making it longer. New length?

8.note: Need to actually double the area

feedback.incorrect: Old area=28. Doubled=56. New length = 56÷4 = 14 m.

practice_r3.three_read_prompt: Read 1: Old area? Read 2: New area? Read 3: Length with same width?

verification.assessment_source: Smarter Balanced Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications 2015

hints: Find old area first, then double, then divide by width.
